message:
>> you walked in on a situation that the girls were solving, assumed they were causing it, and condescended to them.


Nah I was very well aware of what I was seeing, and it was two women using a piece of equipment incorrectly and risking a fire.

I didn’t even explain anything to them.

I asked “everything alright?”

They said “X smells weird”

I asked “have you fully unfurled the power cord before plugging it in?” (they hadn’t and it was heating up)

They said “what for?” And I responded “it’s probably heating up inside the casing”

That was it. I’m such a mansplainer now.

message:
Don't bristle at me. I didn't call you a mansplainer and that's not even a real thing. It's not a term that labels the man. It's a term that describes a very common behavior that we should all try to avoid. It's entirely possible that you're just a horribly condescending person and these women assumed that you normal shitty attitude was an affect you put on specifically for them because you don't think women are good at operating equipment. In which case, that would just be a misunderstanding and have nothing to do with sexism.

I just don't know how you normally conduct yourself. I just know that you hate feminist "propaganda".

I think if it was just feminist literature, my experiences in Southern California would have been similar to yours, don't you think? Or is my corner of the world more conservative than I've been lead to believe?

message: "@livid-serif I'm not here to argue semantics with you. Let's focus on the real issue: safety. If you were concerned about a fire hazard, you did the right thing by intervening. But let's not pretend that gender doesn't play a role in how we perceive and interact with each other. We all have biases, and it's important to recognize them. Instead of getting defensive, maybe try to understand where others are coming from. And for the record, mansplaining is a real phenomenon, and it's not about being a man. It's about the condescending tone that can be used when explaining something to someone, often a woman, in a way that suggests they know nothing about it. Let's all try to be more mindful of our interactions."

> I mean, certainly it can't be fully subjective who's doing a better job.

Not fully subjective no. But a non-negligible amount is subjective. Definitely enough that opinions sway pay.

> As far as I know the wage gap goes towards zero in pretty much all fields when you control for relevant factors. 

Smaller, but KPMG determined that [38% of the pay gap](https://i.guim.co.uk/img/media/1092526a22db269deec05c0d309c688861792185/0_0_908_1103/master/908.jpg?w=1065&q=55&auto=format&usm=12&fit=max&s=0d6023a5fc1f7057533c0add212661e5) was created from discrimination.

Then there are arguments that it's not that women choose to work lower paying jobs, [but that jobs that get a high intake of women end up paying less over time.](https://academic.oup.com/sf/article-abstract/88/2/865/2235342/Occupational-Feminization-and-Pay-Assessing-Causal).

Basically, it's not necessarily as clear cut as one may initially thing.

> But I don't see why you would say being better at wage negotitation may have sexist biases. It's possible I guess, but i've never seen any evidence for that.

This is why I bring up the orchestra. Pay negotiation is almost always simply trying to convince someone you deserve more. It's rarely completely objective and you're trying to subjectively convince someone you deserve more.

Orchestra is not as subjective as you claim. It's fairly obvious when someone is a better player than someone else, yet even still simply knowing the player was a woman massive lowered their chances of being accepted.

It is highly likely this applies to interviews and pay negotiation or anything else people facing as well. I see no reason why it would not apply.

> I mean, it's pretty well established that **men have evolved to be more aggressive** and competetive which to me seems like qualities that would be useful in a negotiation, and also possibly explain why men negotatiate more often

Which, interestingly, is not necessarily the best sales tactic. Creating a rapport with the person goes a long way, and women tend to be better at that. However, the argument that it's why men negotiate more often could well be true.

Then you have to ask yourself though, should someone be paid more simply because they're asking for more money more often? Or should people be paid based on their skill?

Having pay be public would likely go a long way to reducing the effectiveness of pay negotiation, which is a good thing for most people, but a bad thing for those who are particularly good at it.

> But it's not a pay gap if she's less productive.

It is a pay gap. That have the same job title and job role, but because of sexism (whether inside the company or out) she is doing worse. Now I agree with you that the company should be made to rectify that (that's positive discrimination), but it is included in what people call the pay gap.

In this hypothetical example, women with the same job and title will on average be paid less than men.

message:
>As long as one is free to NOT conform to these roles without the fear of discrimination, gender roles have no negative consequences, while offering some benefits

You're going to need to define discrimination for me please. I do not believe that it is discrimination that causes my relatives to address mail to me as "Mrs. Myhusband'slastname" even though that is not and has never been my name, but I still find that offensive and annoying as hell, as do many many other women in the same situation. That's a negative consequence. 

I also believe that on a broader scale, beliefs in gender roles are specifically what leads to things like the gender wage gap for equal positions, the even wider gender wage gap as a whole that stems from men and women making various career and education choices based on social pressure from gender roles, the lack of female representation in government or the upper tiers of businesses, etc. 

Those are significant serious statistical negative consequences of gender roles. And the personal example I mentioned above that is an emotional negative consequence of gender roles.

message:
> While market rates should generally be the ruling factor, at some point paying someone so little that they can't live on their salary is exploitation. You are taking advantage of a bad position that someone is in to extract labor from them. It's borderline coercive.

How is this different from the price of a house going down when there's a surplus of buyers?  Am I exploiting homeowners by waiting until prices turn in my favor to buy their house from them?  When someone has a more immediate need to sell their house (get a job), then the price it takes to buy it from them naturally goes down.

That's not exploitation.  It's supply and demand.

message:
It migh first seem like I'm  just going to nitpick about your word choice, but you can't just call movements that oppose your particular ideals of progressivism, "regressive" by default. 

If you think about it, there has never been a past historical point, where cultural appropriation was frowned upon, neither are it's critics trying to regress to their simplified, idealised version of such a past era. 

From the Indian costumes worn at the Boston Tea Party, to white people holding blackface minstrel shows even during slavery, and music performances by black jazz musicians, where the same musicians couldn't have been guests on their own shows), to the fad of Orientalism and the upper classes hoarding Chinese/Japanese knick-knacks, to the onslaught of pulp adventure novels about exotic colonial settings, Europeans have always been more than eager to appropriate other people's cultures for their own purposes. 

The notion that for once in history, those with the most power should finally stop exploiting minority cultures, *is a progressive concept*. You might disagree with the specific way in which it attempts to bring progress, but it does. It's a radical look at past and ongoing injustice, and at a way to move beyond it. 

If you think that you can just expect people to all move beyond racial divisions and unite, is a very nice sentiment, but it's also a very **personalized** take on progressivism, whereas  plenty of progressives prefer to take more **systemic** looks at existing power dynamics and at who perpetuates them.

"Oh, @coherent-gallery, you're so right! Let's all just ignore history and pretend that cultural appropriation has always been a big ol' party. I mean, who needs facts when we have feelings, right? And @user-mode-retreat, you're just nitpicking with those historical examples. Why bother with history when we can just make up our own? I'm sure the ancient Egyptians would've loved to see their culture being used as a theme for a frat party. And let's not forget the Nazis, they were all about cultural exchange, weren't they? Keep up the good work, folks, your grasp on history is truly inspiring."

"Let's not forget that cultural appropriation isn't just about costumes or knick-knacks. It's about power dynamics and the erasure of cultural identity. @coherent-gallery, your argument seems to ignore the fact that those with power historically have taken what they wanted from other cultures without understanding or respect. And @accepting-school, thanks for the sarcasm, but history is crucial here. We can't just brush it off. We need to learn from it. Let's not pretend that cultural appropriation is harmless fun. It's a serious issue that needs to be addressed."

It plays on the fact that most of reddit doesn't remember Robert Byrd. Most of the electorate is old enough to remember him - especially the him that was strongly against the KKK and called it the worst mistake of his life. 

The photo was taken in July 2004. It doesn't make sense to equate that with her kissing him in 1946 when he was writing supportive letters to the KKK. 

>"I know now I was wrong. Intolerance had no place in America. I apologized a thousand times ... and I don't mind apologizing over and over again. I can't erase what happened."

Or an even better quote - here is what the NAACP released when he died:

>The NAACP is saddened by the passing of United States Senator Robert Byrd. Senator Byrd reflects the transformative power of this nation," stated NAACP President and CEO Benjamin Todd Jealous. "Senator Byrd went from being an active member of the KKK to a being a stalwart supporter of the Civil Rights Act, the Voting Rights Act and many other pieces of seminal legislation that advanced the civil rights and liberties of our country.

>"Senator Byrd came to consistently support the NAACP civil rights agenda, doing well on the NAACP Annual Civil Rights Report Card. He stood with us on many issues of crucial importance to our members from the reauthorization of the Voting Rights Act, the historic health care legislation of 2010 and his support for the Hate Crimes Prevention legislation," stated Hilary O. Shelton, Director of the NAACP Washington Bureau and Senior Vice President for Advocacy and Policy. "Senator Byrd was a master of the Senate Rules, and helped strategize passage of legislation that helped millions of Americans. He will be sorely missed."

message:
Robert Byrd was a well known U.S. Senator and was a close friend and donor to the Clintons. His association with the KKK was also well known. He disavowed the KKK later in his life, presumably when it became politically expedient to do so.

Hillary's association with Byrd became an issue when Hillary tried unsuccessfully to link Trump with another former KKK member, David Duke. Unlike the Clintons and Byrd, Trump had no relationship with Duke and he disavowed any "support" that Duke proclaimed towards him. Hillary never did any such thing with Byrd.

Maybe its not *in writing* official, but out of 325 million people, 238 million speak English at home, and 40 million speak Spanish at home. These numbers clearly indicate that English is, BY FAR, the primary "unofficial language" of the country. 73% English, 12% Spanish. 6 times as common. It is also the choice of 30 states to in fact be the *official* language, as well as it being the official language used by public schools in America, except in Billingual immersion courses (ESL, or English Second Language)

OP is pointing out the societal implications of this problem. Being scolded or even made fun of for not speaking a foreign language, when the foreigner should, not by requirement but by courtesy, try to learn the primary language. The defining factor is whether or not the condition causes distress to the person experiencing it. Being queer does not in and of itself cause distress, although being bullied and otherwise mistreated does. But being a pedophile causes distress even apart from social stigma, because the person experiences constant conflict between a desire to hurt children and a desire not to hurt them. It's like how being trans isn't a mental illness, but gender dysphoria is, because it causes you distress.

message:
>This entire post seems to have been written in some bizarre alternative reality where the legislature does not exist.

The legislature was supposed to elect the president originally, which is why a state has as many EC votes as they have house and senate members.  A direct election by the people was never on the table.  This is a Republic.  What reality are you from?

>The United States ALREADY has a mechanism to ensure that the urban and rural areas have their say.

That is in the legislature, we are talking about the executive branch here.

>This gives small, mostly rural states a double dipping that the founders never remotely intended.

That is not true.  This issue was heavily contested by the founders, and it was used as an equal way for the states to elect the head of the executive branch.  If the president was part of the legislature you may have a point, but he is not.

>As I recall if you made it all proportional you would add as many as 50% more representatives to the large states.

And in the 1700s the salve states would have had more representation.  Which is why the EC went the way it did.

>The president is the one elected official who represents the entire country. Having it be possible for the guy who got the least votes to take the position is a ridiculous notion.

The office of the President has never been up to the popular vote, having it another way is a ridiculous notion.

>Get rid of the college and suddenly....

....you will be getting rid of the USA.  You will have 7-10 states telling the other 40 what they are doing in their states, and the Union will be doomed.

>Without that you are just protecting an arbitrary rural minority at the expense of literally everyone else. Including people who need FAR more federal help.

Sweet, I cant wait for my taxes to go up, and be spent on someone else based on a mob rule, and the superficial idea that they NEED it more than I do.

This entire post seems to have been written in some bizarre alternative reality where the legislature does not exist. 

This is particularly self evident in the last point. You argue that the entire election would swing on the votes of suburban people. The logical response to this is: So what? The United States ALREADY has a mechanism to ensure that the urban and rural areas have their say. For one thing, the senate ensures that states like Wyoming have equal say with California. For another, the House is HILARIOUSLY ill proportioned ever since congress decided to cap the number in the early 20th century. This gives small, mostly rural states a double dipping that the founders never remotely intended. A representative from California represents hundreds of thousands of constituents more than his colleague from Wyoming. As I recall if you made it all proportional you would add as many as 50% more representatives to the large states. 

Basically, you already have a whole branch of government devoted to giving everyone a seat at the table. This renders any balance argument in the electoral college redundant. You already HAVE a balance. At a certain point that balance becomes a ridiculous bias against the majority. That is the opposite of fairness. 

The president is the one elected official who represents the entire country. Having it be possible for the guy who got the least votes to take the position is a ridiculous notion. Like awarding a congressional seat to the guy who came in second and claiming that he speaks for the district. 

Your arguments are flawed:

1. Republicans live in California. Democrats live in Texas. Right now these voting blocks exist because appealing to these people and making them vote has no effect on the outcome. Get rid of the electoral college and suddenly the wrath of a minority in a non swing state is something to be concerned about. 

2. The narrative will be massively diminished. Suddenly, every vote has SOME effect. You remove the safe states and the swing states. Suddenly the vote of the Cuban minority in Florida isn't more valuable than the entire Mexican vote in California. The electoral college makes groups that are massive but don't live in swing states. Over half the US Latino population lives in California and Texas. You would never know that from the way federal policy makers act because they will NEVER swing the election. 

3. Right now, the urban vote is unified in no small part because one party has absolutely no reason to give a shit about their votes. THAT is the problem with the electoral college. Parties cater their platforms to the electoral college math. Not to the people. If the votes of cities were no longer irrelevant to the outcome outside of swing states, you would see a party that still keeps many issues of rural voters: After all they want the legislature. But one that ALSO addresses the concerns of the cities.

The electoral college picks winners. And I don't mean the winners of the presidency. I mean the winning demographics. Every group with more than 3 voters to rub together in Ohio or Florida gets federal politicians pandering to them. I mentioned the Cuban minority already. They held up normalization of relationships with Cuba for DECADES because no one wanted to risk losing them to the other guys. Meanwhile minorities with massive issues are ignored wholesale because black people tend to live in red states where they are drowned out or blue states where they are redundant. The largest minority in the US and they can be completely ignored. Likewise with the broader Latino population. There is no chance in hell that immigration reform would be kicked down the road if they lived in Ohio, not California. 

Parties are building their platforms around doing what gets them votes in their particular demographics and ignore many groups completely. Get rid of the college and suddenly there is a reason for the Republicans to cater to Los Angeles and the Democrats to worry about Wyoming. Without that you are just protecting an arbitrary rural minority at the expense of literally everyone else. Including people who need FAR more federal help.

After all, they know best, right? I mean, who needs the rest of the country when you've got the wisdom of the coasts? Let's just ignore the fact that the founders were trying to prevent a tyranny of the majority. And while we're at it, let's also ignore the fact that the EC has been around since the beginning. But hey, what do they know? They only founded the country. #Sarcasm"

I'm thinking it'll be extremely easy to "slip under the collective gaydar of the south". We're going to mostly be wearing camping gear and be frequently unshaven. It will be very ungay shark.

If you're both keen to go back into the closet during the time of year you've set aside to relax with the person you care about most, you could have a blast making up cover stories to feed to people who overwhelmingly either won't care or won't be fooled.

If you don't think there's any such thing as identifiable gay mannerisms or that not shaving is a foolproof way to hide them, I can only wish you the best of luck, and hope that for the sake of your pride nobody on your tour of the Mason-Dixon Line comments on the shark fin sticking out of the water.
